title: Retention by Use-Case

content.statement: Users stay for specific repeated jobs, not vague value.
content.question: What's the specific repeat job your business does for a member, in their words?
sop: Interview or survey your 5 most loyal customers and ask exactly what task they use your product or service for, in their own words.
List every recurring job your offer performs for someone.
Rate each job's Frequency (times per month), Importance (1-10), and Satisfaction (1-10) (see calculation, page 6).
Identify the single job with the highest combined score, that's your retention anchor.
Redesign onboarding to get new users to that anchor job within their first session, and de-prioritize features tied to low-scoring jobs.
content.explainer: Vague value = vague retention. Specific repeat jobs = sticky retention.
questions: What's the specific repeat job your business does for a member, in their words?
If a customer stopped using you tomorrow, what specific recurring task would they suddenly have to do by hand again?
Which of your features get used once and forgotten, and which get used every single week?

definitions.def: The percentage of customers who keep paying or using a product over time.

content.equation_example: Job A: Frequency 12 uses per month times Importance 9 times Satisfaction 8 equals 864. Job B: Frequency 1 times Importance 6 times Satisfaction 9 equals 54. Higher totals mean that job is what's actually driving retention, protect and build around it. Low scores mean the job is nice-to-have, not sticky, and won't save you if you cut it.
content.closing_statement: Ask what job you're actually being hired for.

caption:
Principle Nº 87: Retention by Use-Case

Users stay for specific repeated jobs, not vague value. Vague value = vague retention.

Ask yourself: What's the specific repeat job your business does for a member, in their words?
